563:
Dr. Abigail Thompson: So, what brings you in today? I see you've had an interesting medical history, let's talk about that sore throat you mentioned.

Patient: Yeah, doc. It's been killing me. I can barely swallow anything without it hurting like hell. Plus, it's worse this time because my voice is also all raspier than usual. I feel like I've been smoking too much, which I haven't, might I add.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: Right, well let's take a look. Say "ahh" for me. It looks a bit red. I'm going to swab your throat to rule out anything bacterial. We'll also need to do a monospot test, just to be sure we're covering all bases.

Patient: Sure thing, doc. Whatever you need.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: Alright, we're going to send these off. I'm pretty sure we're looking at a bacterial infection, so we'll get you started on antibiotics as soon as we get the results back. In the meantime, you might want to try some throat lozenges to keep that inflammation down.

Patient: You mean like those cough drops from last time with my cough? Is it going to be like that?

Dr. Abigail Thompson: Well this time it is more severe but try it anyway, see how it goes. If I were you, I'd also try to stay hydrated and avoid anything too spicy or acidic. And if your sore throat gets worse or you start feeling feverish, come back in, okay?

Patient: Will do, doc. Thanks for always being on it.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: You should be fine if you follow the plan, call me if you have any questions.
564:
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Good Afternoon, what brings you in today?
Patient: Good Afternoon, I've been experiencing some persistent nausea and vomiting.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Okay, how long has this been going on for?
Patient: About three days now.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: How long have you been feeling unwell for?
Patient: I've felt a little off for about a week and a half.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Alright, I'll get someone to organize for you to go to radiology so we can get a look inside with an Ultra Sound.
Patient: Thank you.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: After that, you can wait in reception till I get back to you with results.
565:
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Afternoon, how can I help you today?
Patient: Good afternoon.  I've been having quite a bit of trouble with a sore throat for the past couple of days. It's been quite uncomfortable.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: I see. How severe is the pain? On a scale of 1 to 10, where would you say it is?
Patient: Hmm, maybe a 6. It's not unbearable, but it's persistent and makes it hard to swallow.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Can you tell me if you've had any fever or chills along with the sore throat? Any other symptoms like a cough or runny nose?
Patient: No, just the sore throat. No fever, no cough, nothing like that.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Alright, let's take a look at your throat. Open wide and say "ahh."
[He shines a light and examines the throat.]
Can you tilt your head back and I'll take a quick swab for a throat culture.
Patient: Sure, go ahead.
[After taking the swab.]
Dr. Ethan Wilson: The throat culture result should be ready in a day or so. I can't rule anything out by examining it, but it doesn't look like you have an obvious infection. I'll also order a complete blood count to check for any underlying issues.
Patient: That sounds good, doc.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Until we get the results, I'll prescribe some throat lozenges and suggest gargling with warm salt water to ease the discomfort. Make sure you stay hydrated.
Patient: Will do, thank you, doctor.
Dr.Ethan Wilson: I'll let you know the results as soon as they come in. In the meantime, just take it easy and keep that throat soothed.
566:
Dr. Olivia Martinez: Good evening, I understand you're feeling dizzy?
Patient: Yes, it started a couple hours ago, just came on suddenly.
Dr. Olivia Martinez: I'm sorry to hear that, I can see you're trying to stay strong. Let's find out what's causing this, okay? Can you tell me more about it? Does it happen all the time, or just sometimes?
Patient: It comes and goes, doc. Sometimes I feel like the room is spinning, other times it's just a bit wobbly. I've been trying to stay calm, but it's hard.
Dr. Olivia Martinez: You're doing great. Let's check a few things. Do you have any other symptoms? Headache, nausea, anything like that?
Patient: Just a bit of nausea, nothing too bad.
Dr. Olivia Martinez: Alright, let's run some tests to figure out what's going on and get it sorted
Patient: That sounds good, doc. Anything to stop this spinning. This happened once before when my grandson was telling his stories, but this time it's much worse
Dr. Olivia Martinoz: Don't worry, I'll find out what's going on and how to help you
567:
Dr Sophia Patel: Good morning. I understand you're here because of a cough?
Patient: Yes, it's been bothering me for the past couple of weeks.
Dr Sophia Patel: I see. Can you tell me more about it? When did it start, and has it been consistent?
Patient: Started about two weeks ago. It's worse in the mornings and evenings. I've been coughing up a bit of phlegm too.
Dr Sophia Patel: I'm sorry to hear that, thank you for sharing. I'll need to examine you. Can you lay down on the bed and let me listen to your chest?
Patient: Sure, go ahead.
Dr Sophia Patel: Alright, take a few deep breaths for me. Does it hurt when you take a deep breath? Do you notice any wheezing?
Patient: No, just feels uncomfortable sometimes. Not much wheezing.
Dr Sophia Patel: Okay, that's helpful to know. Sometimes patients notice other symptoms such as shortness of breath. Have you experienced any of that?
Patient: No shortness of breath. Just the cough and the phlegm.
Dr Sophia Patel: Very well, I think we should conduct some tests to better understand what's going on. We'll start with a pulmonary function test and a chest X-ray. If necessary, we might also need to do a sputum culture.
Patient: Alright, whatever you think is best. I just want to get this cough sorted out.
Dr Sophia Patel: Of course. I'll get the nursing team to take you to the lab. We'll review the results and I'll let you know what we find out.
Patient: Thank you, doc. I appreciate your help.
568:
[SYSTEM_MESSAGE]

Dr. Emily Carter: Good afternoon. This is Dr. Emily Carter calling from the hospital. I have your test results from your recent visit for nausea and vomiting.
Patient: Hey Doc. Was just getting worried. Any news?
Dr. Emily Carter: Your CT scan results came back normal.
Patient: Great! So what's next?
Dr. Emily Carter: Given your previous visit for a cough, where we managed it with aspirin and rest, I suggest you continue to monitor your symptoms. If the nausea and vomiting persist or worsen, come back to see us. Otherwise, just keep an eye on things and try to rest.
Patient: Okay, I will. Thank you, Doctor.
Dr. Emily Carter: You're welcome. Take care, and feel free to call if you have any more questions.
Patient: Will do. Bye.
Dr. Emily Carter: Goodbye
569:
Dr. Isabella Nguyen: Good afternoon. I understand you're here because of back pain? Let's get to the bottom of this. Can you describe the pain for me? Where exactly does it hurt?
Patient: Hello Doctor. Yes, the pain is quite severe, felt mainly around my lower back.
Patient: It's been going on for about a week now, and it's worse when I move around.
Dr. Isabella Nguyen: I see. Have you had any falls or injuries recently that might have caused this?
Patient: No, nothing like that. It just came out of nowhere.
Dr. Isabella Nguyen: Hmm... well, let's get you set up for an MRI and an electromyography. This'll help us figure out what's going on.
Patient: Okay, Doctor. I appreciate your help.
Dr. Isabella Nguyen: I'll make sure we get to the source of your pain, don't worry. I'll keep you posted. Let's get the tests done as soon as possible.
570:
Dr. Abigail Thompson: Good afternoon. I heard you're experiencing some dizziness.
Patient: Yes, that's right. It's been going on for a couple of days now.
Dr. Abigail Thompson: I see. Let's try to figure this out. Have you had any episodes before this?
Patient: No, this is the first time it's happened.
Patient: Sometimes I feel a bit off balance, like the room is spinning.
Dr. Abigail Thompson: Have you noticed anything that triggers these episodes?
Patient: Not really. It seems to come and go randomly.
Dr. Abigail Thompson: Okay. Let's run a few tests to get a clearer picture. We'll start with a CT scan and an EEG to check your brain and neurological activity.
Patient: Sure, whatever you think is best.
Dr. Abigail Thompson: I know it might feel a bit overwhelming, but we'll take it one step at a time. Sound good?
Patient: Yes, thank you Doctor.
571:
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Good morning. I hear you're feeling dizzy? Let's figure this out. Can you describe your symptoms?

 Patient: Good morning, doctor. Yes, I've been feeling dizzy intermittently over the past few days. It seems to come and go, and there's no specific trigger I can identify, it does not come with head aches or any nausea, which the internet suggests usually comes with dizziness.

 Dr. Ethan Wilson: I see. Have you noticed any other symptoms? Like nausea, vomiting, or headaches?

 Patient: No, none of those. It's just the dizziness, it comes and goes and I just have to sit down

 Dr. Ethan Wilson: Alright, I'll need to run some tests to pinpoint the cause. We'll start with an MRI and a CT scan. These should help us rule out any major issues. These should come with an outpatient review, so I will tell my secretary to schedule that.

 Patient: Okay, that sounds good. Do you need me to do anything specific before the tests?

 Dr. Ethan Wilson: Nothing specific. Just try to stay hydrated and avoid any stressful activities. I'll have the results as soon as possible. We'll take it from there.
572:
Dr. Sophia Patel: Good morning. I understand you're feeling lightheaded today. Can you tell me more about when this started and what you were doing at the time?
Patient: Good morning, Doctor. Yes, I've been feeling lightheaded for the past few hours. I was sitting at my desk working when it started.
Dr. Sophia Patel: Have you experienced anything like this before?
Patient: Yes, a few weeks ago I had a similar incident but it wasn't as severe. I was diagnosed with Normal.
Dr. Sophia Patel: Were you given any treatment for that?
Patient: I was prescribed aspirin and told to get some rest.
Dr. Sophia Patel: How long did the symptoms last that time?
Patient: It only lasted for a few minutes. This time, it's been going on for a couple of hours.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I see. Have you taken any aspirin today?
Patient: No, I haven't.
Dr. Sophia Patel: Alright. Just to be thorough, I'd like to run some tests. We'll start with a magnetic resonance imaging scan to rule out any potential issues. Is that okay with you?
Patient: Yes, that sounds fine.
Dr. Sophia Patel: Great. Let's get that scheduled. In the meantime, let's get you some aspirin, just to ease any underlying discomfort. How does that sound?
Patient: That sounds good. Thank you, Doctor.
573:
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Good afternoon. I hear you've been experiencing nausea and vomiting today.
Patient: Yes, it started a couple of hours ago. It's quite severe.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: I see. Have you had any fever or abdominal pain along with it?
Patient: No, just the nausea and vomiting. It's been pretty constant.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: We will check it out thoroughly to be absolutely certain. Just wanted to ask. You have not had any recent travel history or exposure to anything unusual?
Patient: No, nothing like that.
 Dr. Hannah Johnson: Just to give you a better picture I have some background information from a previous visit. When you had dizziness last time, what exactly did you feel like?
Patient: Oh, yes. It was more like feeling lightheaded, like the room was spinning. It was strange.
 Dr. Hannah Johnson: Have you experienced any other symptoms along with that?
Patient: Nothing specific, just a general feeling of disorientation.
 Dr. Hannah Johnson: And lastly you've mentioned you have had a case of a Tumor how did you find out about the tumor? And are you still undergoing treatment for the tumor?
Patient: The diagnosis was made after some scans in the previous visit. I have been taking paclitaxel for the treatment, as recommended by my previous doctor.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Do you experience any pain in the abdomen?
Patient: No not at all. It's just the nausea and vomiting.
 Dr. Hannah Johnson: Ok. Just as a last question has vomiting left any traces? Like discolored? Or something unusual?
Patient: No, it's just regular color. Nothing unusual, just very uncomfortable.
 Dr. Hannah Johnson: Okay, let's order a CT scan to get a clearer picture of what might be causing these symptoms. It'll help us rule out any potential issues. Does that sound good to you?
Patient: Yes, that sounds fine. Let's get to the bottom of this.
574:
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Good day, how can I assist you today?
Patient: Afternoon doctor. I've been feeling a bit lightheaded, it seems to come and go.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: I see. That's concerning. Have you experienced this before?
Patient: Yes, a few times, but it's been more frequent this week. The last time you said it was normal.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Right, let's take a closer look this time. We might need to run some tests. Have you had any other symptoms along with the lightheadedness?
Patient: Not really, though I've had some nausea and vomiting before, but it was just one time.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Nausea and vomiting, interesting. We should consider that too. Sometimes, these symptoms can be interconnected. Let's start with an MRI and a CT scan to get a clearer picture. We might also need an EEG to check your brain activity.
Patient: An EEG? That sounds serious.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: It's just a precaution, but we want to be thorough. Your health is our top priority, and we want to make sure we understand what's going on. Would you like a nurse to help make you more comfortable before we start?
575:
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Good morning. I understand you've been experiencing nausea and vomiting. Let's start with what time did these symptoms began?
Patient: Good morning. They started a few hours ago. I was feeling fine before lunch, but then it all came on pretty suddenly.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Have you been nauseous before today?
Patient: Not that I can think of. I feel awful. I can't seem to find anything that settles my stomach.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Did you eat or drink something recently that might have upset your stomach?
Patient: I don't think so, I ate chicken with gravy, but I've eaten it before and have been fine.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Well let's get an ultrasound with my team to see if we can figure out what is going on.  Have you had any other symptoms besides nausea and vomiting?
Patient: No, nothing else. Just this awful feeling in my stomach. I'm starting to get a bit worried.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: I understand, but let's take things one step at a time. How about I get the ultrasound and then we can do a blood test to check for any electrolyte imbalances, just to be thorough. Sound good?
Patient: Yeah that's great, let's find out.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Excellent, my team and I will get this taken care of and be back to you with the results as soon as we can. Try to stay calm, let's just take this one step at a time.
Patient: Thanks, doctor.
576:
Dr. Olivia Martinez: Hello there, I understand you're here because you're experiencing some diarrhea?

Patient: Oh, yes doctor. It's been quite unpleasant. I've had it for about two days now.

Dr. Olivia Martinez: I'm really sorry to hear that. How's the rest of your health been? Any other symptoms besides the diarrhea?

Patient: No, just this. Everything else feels normal. I still have plenty of energy.

Dr. Olivia Martinez: That's good to hear. Sometimes it can be just a passing bug, but it can be very uncomfortable. Have you noticed any patterns with it? Any specific foods that might have triggered it?

Patient: Hmm, I don't think so. I ate the usual things, nothing special really.

Dr. Olivia Martinez: Okay. It could just be something that your body needs to flush out. But just to be sure, I'd like to run a stool test to rule out any infections or something more serious.

Patient: Sure, whatever you think is best.

Dr. Olivia Martinez: Just a quick and simple test, but it can give us a good idea of what's going on. I'll get the nurse to take care of that for you. In the meantime, drink plenty of fluids to stay hydrated—water, electrolyte drinks, or even clear broths.

Patient: Of course, I'll do that. Thank you for your help, doctor.

Dr. Olivia Martinez: You're welcome. I'll keep you updated as soon as we get the results. It's important to let us know if the symptoms get worse or if you start to feel unwell in any other way. Take it easy for now.
577:
Dr. Abigail Thompson: Good afternoon. So, you've mentioned you've got a sore throat? How long's it been bothering you?

Patient: Hi, it's been about a week now. I just thought it might be a common cold, but it's been so persistent.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: I see. And how has your energy level been otherwise? Any other symptoms like fever or coughing?

Patient: Actually, I've been feeling a bit run down, but no fever. Just this annoying sore throat. It's making it tough to eat and sleep.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: Alright, let's run some tests to figure this out. I want to see if we can pinpoint the cause, whether it's viral or bacterial.

Patient: Sure, doctor. Whatever it takes to get rid of this thing. I had a bout of diarrhea last year with no underlying cause. They gave me some Aspirin for the next few days.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: You'll be fine. I need to swab your throat. The results will tell us a lot. But judging by your symptoms we should run a monospot test too.

Patient: What's that for?

Dr. Abigail Thompson: If you're wondering, it's because this is the best test to rule out glandular fever, it's likely what you're experiencing.

Patient: Anything to get rid of this misery. Should I rest?

Dr. Abigail Thompson: Rest is good. In the meantime if the throat gets worse, start with some Aspirin every few hours but don't overdo it. You were prescribed Aspirin before so we know it works for you.

Patient: Got it. Thanks, doc!
578:
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Well, you're here again. What brings you in today? More than just looking tired?
Patient: Hi Doc. Yeah, I've been feeling really tired all the time. I mean, really tired.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Do you feel anything else? Headache, fever, anything like that?
Patient: No, just the tiredness. It's like I can't get enough sleep no matter how much I rest.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Have you been sleeping okay at night? No trouble falling asleep or staying asleep?
Patient: No, I sleep like a baby. I just wake up feeling like I haven't slept at all.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Alright, let's run some tests. We'll check your electrolyte levels and your thyroid function. Sometimes, fatigue can be a sign of something more serious.
Patient: Sure, Doc. Whatever you think is best.
Dr. Ethan Wilson: It might be necessary to adjust your treatment based on the results. Sometimes we have to try different things to find what works.
579:
Dr. William Brown: Good day, I see you've got your temperature up.
Patient: Yeah, I've been feeling really under the weather for the past 24 hours. Thought I'd better come in and get it checked out.
Dr. William Brown: Right. So it's been a full day of fever. Any other symptoms?
Patient: A bit of aching in my muscles and joints, just an overall sense of being unwell.
Dr. William Brown: Alright, let's get some tests done. We'll start with a Complete Blood Count, Urinalysis, and a Blood Culture to see if there's anything serious going on.
Patient: Okay, sounds good. I hope it's nothing too bad.
Dr. William Brown: Me too but right now I am not making any assumptions. The quicker we check these out, the sooner we'll have answers.
580:
Dr. James Anderson: Hey there, good to see you, again,  just a couple of months ago. You had a fever right?
Patient: Yeah, they said i didn't have anything.
Dr. James Anderson: Well that was a while ago. So, today you said your issue is pain?
Patient: Yeah, I've been really constipated for the past few days. It's quite uncomfortable.
Dr. James Anderson: Okay, well let's tackle this. First, I'll get a few basic tests done. We'll start with an anoscopy to see if there's any obvious blockage and I'll get you a abdominal x-ray to confirm its a blockage.
Patient: Sounds good, doc.
Dr. James Anderson: Right. So while we wait for the results lets see what kind of diet you have?
Patient: Pretty normal, not much in terms of vegetables and stuff.
Dr. James Anderson: You should get more in there, fiber will definitely help... let me know if it gets worse. We might need to do a colonoscopy.
Patient: A colonoscopy? Isn't that a bit extreme?
Dr. James Anderson: We'll see about that, sometimes I like to be a bit thorough.
581:
Dr. Emily Carter: Good afternoon. I understand you're here because of a sore throat?
 Patient: Yes, it's been bothering me for a few days now and it's getting quite uncomfortable.
 Dr. Emily Carter: I see. How long have you had these symptoms, and what exactly does the discomfort feel like?
 Patient: Well, it began with a bit of hoarseness, but now it's a constant scratchy feeling. It's tough to swallow, and it feels like there's something stuck in my throat.
 Dr. Emily Carter: Have you noticed any other symptoms? Like coughing, or maybe a bit of a fever?
 Patient: No, just the sore throat.
 Dr. Emily Carter: Okay. Let's take a look. Open your mouth wide and say "ah." *pauses* Your neck doesn't seem swollen, and there's no pus or redness in the throat. I'm going to swab your throat just to rule out any infections that might be missed. *Collects sample* How have you been feeling otherwise? Any issues with breathing or tightness in the chest?
 Patient: No, I feel fine other than this sore throat.
 Dr. Emily Carter: Well, your throat looks clear, and the swab should confirm that. If it's just a viral infection, it should clear up on its own in a few days. Just make sure to stay hydrated and avoid irritants like smoking or spicy foods.
 Patient: Thank you, doc.
 Dr. Emily Carter: You're very welcome. If your symptoms worsen or if you develop a fever, give us a call. Otherwise, come back in if it doesn't get better in a week or so.
 Patient: I will, thank you.
582:
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Alright, what brings you in today?

Patient: It's my knee. It's been bothering me a lot lately, hurts pretty bad.

Dr. Ethan Wilson: I see. Let's take a look. Can you tell me more about the pain? Is it constant or does it come and go?

Patient: It comes and goes, mainly when I put pressure on it or move around too much.

Dr. Ethan Wilson: Got it. On a scale of one to ten, how would you rate the pain right now?

Patient: Probably a six or seven when it's bad.

Dr. Ethan Wilson: Alright, let's order an MRI to get a better look inside. And we'll also do an arthrocentesis if you don't mind so we can check the knee fluid for possible conditions. Hopefully, we can figure this out.

Patient: The MRI? That didn't go so well with my sore throat check up, I had to drink that nasty dye.

Dr. Ethan Wilson: Unfortunately I'm afraid of that again this time also. Have you noticed any redness or swelling?
583:
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Good afternoon. I understand you're here because of back pain?
Patient: Yes, Doctor. It's been quite persistent.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: I see. How long has this been going on?
Patient: About a week now. It's been getting progressively worse.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: I'm sorry to hear that.  We need to figure out what's causing it. Have you had any recent falls or injuries?
Patient: No, nothing like that. It just started one day.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Okay.  Have you been experiencing any muscle weakness along with the pain?
Patient: Yes, I noticed my left leg feels a bit weaker than usual.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Alright, that's important to note. Let's take a closer look. I might suggest an electromyography to better understand what's happening. How does that sound?
Patient: Whatever you think is best, Doctor.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: We'll take good care of you. Meanwhile, do you mind if we check your previous visits?
Patient: Not at all.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Thanks. I see from your records there was one visit two months ago for a sore throat, it seemed inconsequential?
Patient: Yes, that was just a false alarm.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Ok I see you were prescribed some aspirin, you mentioned the pain had got worse, are you still experiencing that?
Patient: Unfortunately, yes.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Alright, that was four months ago, the other visit was seven months ago and was for a knee injury?
Patient: Yes, I got a clean bill of health.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Right. I will consult with my colleagues to try to get to the bottom of what's happening with your back.

Patient: I appreciate it, Doctor. I've been quite uncomfortable.
584:
Dr. William Brown: Good morning. I hear you're feeling fatigued.
Patient: Yes, Doctor. It's been going on for a few weeks now. I'm not sure what's causing it.
Dr. William Brown: Have you noticed any other symptoms? Changes in appetite? Weight loss?
Patient: No, not really. But I have had a few issues over the last year. I had a sore throat a few months back, and before that, I was dealing with some pretty bad knee and back pain.
Dr. William Brown: You had a knee tumor. You must keep me updated on that.
Patient: I am on medication, but it's been hard. The fatigue is making it even harder to keep up with everything.
Dr. William Brown: That's understandable. Let's see what we can find out. I suspect we'll do a complete blood count or something like that to get a fuller picture. We've done blood tests before but I want something more thorough.
Patient: I'm up for anything. I just want to feel better. I get by day to day but there is a toll it's taking on me.
Dr. William Brown: I appreciate your cooperation. We'll figure this out. You should go for it as soon as you can.
Patient: Well, thank you, Doctor. I appreciate you taking the time to see me.
Dr. William Brown: You're welcome. I'll make sure to keep you updated on the results. Good day.
585:
Dr. Sophia Patel: Good evening, I see here that you've mentioned you're experiencing a sore throat. Is it something you've been dealing with before?
Patient: Yes, ma'am, it's been a recurring issue over the past few years. I usually get it during the winter months.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I see. Have you noticed there are any other symptoms accompanying the sore throat? Any fever or swelling in your throat? And have you been dealing with anything new that you haven't mentioned before?
Patient: No fever or swelling, but I had a feeling this was different, so that's why I came in. I usually try to manage it at home with over-the-counter remedies.
Dr. Sophia Patel: Okay, we'll get to the bottom of this. Since this is not the first time you've experienced this, let's review some of your previous visits. It seems like you've had a sore throat before, as well as some knee pain, back pain, and fatigue.
Knee pain! Is this what you were referring to?
Dr. Sophia Patel: And for now, let's go about testing the throat. We'll perform a monospot test and a throat culture. These will help us determine if there's an infection causing your sore throat.
Patient: Alright, whatever you think is best. I trust your judgment
586:
Dr. Sophia Patel: Good afternoon. I understand you're here because you're experiencing some chest pain?
Patient: Yes, it's been going on for a few hours now. It's quite uncomfortable.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I'm sorry to hear that. Let me take a closer look. Can you describe the pain? Is it sharp, dull, or something else?
Patient: It's more dull, but it feels like a heavy weight on my chest. It comes and goes, but it's been getting worse.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I see. Have you had any other symptoms aside from the chest pain? Such as shortness of breath, nausea, or sweating  ?
Patient: A bit of shortness of breath, yes. I haven't felt nauseous or sweaty.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I'll start by doing a full physical.  Maybe do a Chest X-ray as well, to rule out a possible cause of the pain. Afterwards I'd like to check for markers of heart disease.
Patient: Sounds good to me. Whatever you think is best, Doctor.
Dr. Sophia Patel: We will get to the bottom of it I am sure.
587:
Dr. Michael Davis: So what brings you in today? You look a bit flushed.
Patient: I've had this high fever for the past few days. It won't go away.
Dr. Michael Davis: You're 57, right? Any other symptoms?
Patient: Just feeling pretty exhausted. Why so many questions?
Dr. Michael Davis: We need to rule out some serious stuff. How long have you had these symptoms?
Patient: Like I said, a few days. I can't sleep because of this fever. Do you think it is serious?
Dr. Michael Davis: Let's not jump to conclusions. I'll order some tests, starting with a CBC. Your previous visit with chest pain was quite serious and you had elevated lipid levels, just to rule out any connections. I do want to check you for any other anomalies.
Patient: Just keep me in the loop, Doc.
Dr. Michael Davis: Standard procedure. We'll start here and see where it leads. Have you been taking any medication recently?
Patient: Just some ibuprofen for my chest pains in the past, none for this fever.
Dr. Michael Davis: Alright, we'll start with the CBC and go from there, just to be safe.
Patient: Okay, thanks Doc.
588:
Dr. Olivia Martinez: I've reviewed your medical history and seen the results of your recent tests. Can you tell me more about the syncope you experienced?

Patient: Doc, this is the third time this has happened; I lost consciousness and then woke up feeling shaky and dizzy. It's scary stuff.

Dr. Olivia Martinez: I understand how frightening this must be. Given your medical history, we need to rule out some potential causes. Do you recall any unusual physical activity or emotional stress before the episode?

Patient: Well, I've been under a lot of stress at work, and I did have that incident with chest pain a couple of years back.

Dr. Olivia Martinez: Right, I see. Both the EEG and CT scan came back normal, but your MRI showed some abnormalities. We need to consider that this could be related to your blood conditions.

Patient: Blood conditions? You mean like the leukemia?

Dr. Olivia Martinez: Yes, there might be a connection. Since we need a clearer picture, I'll consult with the hematologist to get their input. Would you mind if we do another complete blood count? Sometimes, changes in blood counts can give us more insight.
589:
Dr. Emily Carter: Good afternoon. I understand you're here because of back pain?

Patient: Yes, it's been getting worse over the past few days. I can barely get out of bed sometimes.

Dr. Emily Carter: I see. Can you tell me more about the pain? Is it sharp or more of a dull ache?

Patient: It's more of a sharp pain that radiates down my leg. It usually hurts the most when I try to stand up.

Dr. Emily Carter: Have you had any recent injuries or falls that might have caused this?

We need to treat this quickly to manage the pain and prevent further damage. Do you remember what you last ate or if you changed anything in your diet?

Patient: Now that you mention it, I had a few steaks and shellfish over the past week. Do you think that could be related?

Dr. Emily Carter: Good afternoon. I understand you're here because you've been experiencing a headache?

Dr. Emily Carter: I'm going to need to run some tests to determine the cause. First, I want to do a Magnetic Resonance Imaging scan to get a detailed look at your brain.
Patient: Okay, that sounds reasonable. What exactly will this test tell us?
Dr. Emily Carter: An MRI can help us identify any abnormalities in the brain that might be causing your headache. It's a non-invasive procedure that uses magnetic fields and radio waves to create detailed images. The results will give us a clearer picture of what's going on.
Patient: Alright, I understand. What do I need to do to prepare for the MRI?
Dr. Emily Carter: There's not much preparation needed. You'll need to remove any metal objects or jewelry, and you should wear comfortable clothing without metal zippers or fasteners. The scan itself will take about 30 to 60 minutes, during which you'll lie still on a table that slides into the MRI machine.
Patient: That doesn't sound too bad. When can I expect the results?
Dr. Emily Carter: Typically, the results will be available within a few hours. Once we have them, we can discuss the next steps. In the meantime, if your headache worsens or if you experience any new symptoms, please let me know immediately.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: Good afternoon. I see you're here because of back pain. Can you tell me more about when it started and what makes it worse?

Patient: It started suddenly about two days ago. It's really bad when I sit for too long or try to stand up from a chair. I've had some issues in the past, like that time with the chest pain, but this feels different. I don't know if it's related to my anemia or something else.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: I understand. It sounds quite uncomfortable. You mentioned past issues with anemia and that chest pain episode. Those are definitely important to consider. Given your history, it's crucial we take a thorough approach. I'd like to run a few tests to confirm that everything is functioning correctly. What we'll do first is an electromyography test.

Patient: An electromyography? What exactly does that do?

Dr. Hannah Johnson: It's a test that measures the electrical activity in your muscles. It helps us identify if there's any nerve or muscle issue contributing to the back pain. It's noninvasive and quite straightforward. Would you like me to walk you through the procedure?

Patient: Yes, please. It sounds a bit technical, but I'm okay with it. I just want to know what's going on.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: Of course. We'll place small electrodes on your skin, and the machine will measure the electrical signals produced by your muscles. It might feel a bit odd, but it shouldn't be painful. We'll also need to monitor your symptoms closely, especially since you've experienced nausea and vomiting before. Sometimes, these symptoms can be linked to issues that cause pain or other discomforts in different parts of the body.

Patient: So, what happens after the test?

Dr. Hannah Johnson: Once we have the results, we'll discuss them and see if there are any underlying conditions that need attention. If the electromyography comes back normal, we'll explore other possible causes and treatments. Sometimes back pain can be managed with simple lifestyle changes, physical therapy, or sometimes mild painkillers like Aspirin .In the meantime, try to keep moving gently, avoid heavy lifting, and let your body rest when you can.

Patient: Okay, that sounds good. I'll do my best to follow your advice. I'm just glad you're taking this seriously. Thank you, Dr. Johnson.

835:
Dr. James Anderson: Well, I see you're here because you're experiencing some abdominal pain?

Patient: Yes, it's been a real issue these past few days. It's a sharp pain.

Patient: Can you also check for liver problems? My brother had issues with his, and I wonder if it might be something similar.

Dr. James Anderson: Okay, we can certainly look into that. Let's start with an ultrasound to get a better idea of what's going on. It'll help us determine if there are any gallstones or other issues in your abdomen. If the issue is gallstones we can schedule a treatment plan.

Patient: That sounds good. I'll do whatever you recommend. I hope it's not serious.

Dr. James Anderson: We'll get to the bottom of this. As soon as we have the results from the ultrasound, we can decide on the next steps. We need to be thorough, so we might need to repeat the same test in a few days to be sure of the results.  The ultrasound can tell us if there are gallstones or any other blockage that might be causing this discomfort. Once we have that information, we can address the problem.

Patient: How  long is the ultrasound going to take doctor?

Dr. James Anderson:  The ultrasound should take about 20 to 30 minutes.  It's non-invasive and painless, so you should be comfortable throughout. There's no need for any preparation on your part, either. We will just move a small device over your abdomen, using some gel to help with the imaging. If the pain gets worse in the meantime, just let us know  immediately.

Patient: Okay, I can handle that. Can you tell me more about what might be causing this pain? This is the first time I have had any of this, so I am not a really experienced with this type of problem

Dr. James Anderson: Absolutely. Gallstones are one of the more common issues we see with abdominal pain, especially in someone of your age. This is when small stones form in your gallbladder, which is located  under your liver. Sometimes these stones can block the ducts that carry bile from your gallbladder to your small intestine, causing pain and inflammation. This has also happened to your brother

Patient: Is this something that can be treated? I don't want to let it go untreated for too long

Dr. James Anderson:  We can treat gallstones, and there are different options depending on your specific situation. Some people may need surgery to remove the gallbladder, while others can manage it with medications and lifestyle changes. If the ultrasound shows gallstones, we will proceed with scheduling a procedure to address it. In the meantime, if you experience any severe pain, nausea, or vomiting, you should come back to the ER immediately.

Dr. William Brown: I see. Have you had any recent changes in your diet or medications? Any recent travel history that you could remember?

Patient: No, my diet is the same as always. I went to the local lake two weeks ago and we had some cooked meats with some canned peas and pasta salad

Dr. William Brown: I see. It could be related to that.  Has it been occurring multiple times a day? Are there any additional symptoms you've been experiencing?

Patient: Yes, its been multiple times a day.  I haven't noticed any blood, but I've had some cramping. My bowel movements are a bit watery and are not very formed.

Dr. William Brown: The last few times you came to the hospital the diagnosis was either Normal or Abnormal? That doesn't usually happen unless you have some type of disease that is still un-diagnosed.

Dr. William Brown: Okay, I will.   The test is a bit pricey though. While that is being processed, I will also request an electrolyte panel and a complete blood count so you will need to provide a blood sample.  While we wait for the results I will suggest some lifestyle changes.

Patient: I have been prescribed Metronidazole,  Ibuprofen, Diclofenac, Tramadol, and some amoxicin antibiotics.  I am also in need for  some Aspirin.  I don't understand why they put me on so many strong pain killers, when I get pain every day and only take Aspirin.

Dr. William Brown: With your medical records it appears that you have undergone surgery in the past, and the list of medications you are on are consistent with someone who had that procedure. Your latest complaint and the prescribed medicines make little sense together. The stool test from the lab is a test for blood in stool. I can also prescribe you some Aspirin if that is what you need, but I will also have to add something to your current regime as Aspirin can interact with your medication.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: Well, let's address this fatigue. I'll order thyroid function tests, and if you resume your cardiac medication I'd like you to start adding some vegetables, a banana, and maybe an egg to your diet. You need more nutrients to keep going.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: Good, I am happy to hear that, I will ensure your tests are ordered now.

Dr. Ethan Wilson: Alright, let's review your MRI results. The MRI scan seems to indicate the presence of a brain tumor.
Patient: That seems awful. But how did it happen?
Dr. Ethan Wilson: Tumor growth can be spontaneous. But it's surprising to tell you as you've been quite active so the EEG was added to the list to ensure that isn't the case, but it seems to indicate that your brain is functioning normally.

Dr. Ethan Wilson: No, but you should keep coming in for consistent checkups. There still are tests we need to perform, so your records will be updated, and a plan will be drawn to manage this.

 I know you're not a fan of medication but we have to make sure you're comfortable while we do this because constipation can make people feel unwell but can also lead to other things developing. Patient: I dunno, Doc. Never really paid attention to that. But yeah, my sister had something like this.
Dr. Emily Carter: Alright, that's helpful. Have you been taking any medications or painkillers?
Patient: Just the usual stuff. Tylenol sometimes.
Dr. Emily Carter: Right. I think an MRI might help us understand better. Have you had one before?
Patien: No, haven't had one.
Dr. Emily Carter: This would give us a clearer picture of what might be causing the headaches. Do you have any questions about the procedure?
Patient: Not really, doc. Just do what you gotta do.
Dr. Emily Carter: This will be a quick and painless scan. We'll take a look at the results and see what we can find. I'll make sure you're comfortable throughout.
Patient: Alright, let's get this over with.
Dr. Emily Carter: You'll be fine. In the meantime, you should try to stay calm and rest. If the pain worsens, please don't hesitate to call.
Patient: Will do, Doc. Thanks.
967:
Dr. Isabella Nguyen: Good afternoon. I understand you're here because you're experiencing back pain?

Patient: Yeah, it's been bothering me for a few days now. It's really painful, doctor.

Dr. Isabella Nguyen: I see. On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate the pain? Also, have you had any similar issues before?

Patient: It's probably an 8 right now. It's hard to move. I did have a headache a few days ago, but it went away after I took some aspirin and rested.

Dr. Isabella Nguyen: Let's take a look. Can you point to where it hurts the most? It's good that we can rule out a headache at least. I want to order an X-ray and an MRI to get a better look at what's going on.

Patient: Do you think it's serious?

Dr. Isabella Nguyen: It could be. With your age and the severity of the pain, we need to rule out anything significant. The X-ray and MRI will give us a clearer picture. In the meantime, I'll prescribe some painkillers to help you manage the discomfort. We'll go from there.

Patient: Alright, I guess I don't have much of a choice. I just hope it's nothing too bad.
968:
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Good evening, thanks for coming in today. I understand you've been experiencing nausea and vomiting?

Patient: Yeah, it's been pretty rough. I've been feeling sick all day.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: I'm sorry to hear that. On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate your pain or discomfort right now?

Patient: It's probably about a 7. My stomach just feels awful.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: Okay, let's get this sorted out. To better understand what's going on, I think we should do some tests. Would you be okay with an ultrasound and an electrolyte panel?

Patient: Sure, if that's what's necessary.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: Excellent, thank you for being so cooperative. I'll order those tests right away. In the meantime, let's discuss your hydration. Have you been able to keep anything down today?

Patient: Not really. I've tried, but it just comes right back up.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: I see. It's important we get some fluids into you. We might need to consider IV hydration.

Patient: Okay, if that's what you think is best.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: I do. Let's get this started. In a bit, I'd like to run a CT scan as well, just to be thorough. We should be able to get all this done quickly to figure out what's causing your symptoms.

Patient: That sounds good. Do whatever you need to do to help me feel better.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: I appreciate your understanding. Once we get the results back, we can discuss the next steps and get you feeling better soon.

Patient: Thank you, Doctor. I really appreciate it.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: you're welcome. The team and I will be with you every step of the way.
969:
Dr. Emily Carter: Hello, you seem to be feeling a bit unwell. Is your cough getting any better?
Patient: Not really, it's gotten worse in the past week. It started as a minor cough but now it's getting quite painful.
Dr. Emily Carter: I see. How long have you had it for?
Patient: About two weeks now. It comes and goes, but it's mostly getting worse.
Dr. Emily Carter: I'm sorry to hear that. Do you have any other symptoms, like fever or shortness of breath?
Patient: Yes, I've been feeling a bit feverish and it gets hard to breathe sometimes, especially when I'm active.
Dr. Emily Carter: Alright, we'll need to run a few tests to figure out what's going on. Your last visit you came in for some nausea and vomiting. Is there any connection?
Patient: Possibly, I haven't really thought about it to be honest.
Dr. Emily Carter: Let's start with a chest X-ray and maybe a sputum culture. Those should help us understand what's happening. You might need to get rested from being too active.
970:
Dr. Sophia Patel: Good morning. I understand you're here because you've been having trouble with constipation? I'm Dr. Patel, and I'm here to help.
Patient: Yes, that's right. It's been going on for a few days now.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I see. Can you tell me more about what you've been experiencing?
Patient: Well, I haven't had a bowel movement in about three days. I've been eating a lot of fiber and drinking plenty of water, but nothing seems to be helping.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I appreciate you trying those remedies first. Have you noticed any blood in your stool or any abdominal pain?
Patient: No, nothing like that. Just the discomfort of not being able to go.
Dr. Sophia Patel: Alright. Let's do a physical exam and maybe run a few tests, including an anoscopy and colonoscopy, to get a better idea of what's going on.
Patient: Okay, I can do that. I just want to feel better soon.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I understand. We'll do our best to figure this out quickly. Have you had any recent illnesses or infections?
Patient: A few weeks back I was treated for pneumonia.
Dr. Sophia Patel: Were you prescribed any antibiotics?
Patient: Yes, I was given Amoxicillin, Azithromycin, and Clarithromycin. Why do you ask?
Dr. Sophia Patel: Sometimes antibiotics can disrupt your gut flora and lead to digestive issues. It's important to consider all possibilities. Let's get those tests scheduled. I'll also provide you with some recommendations on how to manage constipation in the meantime.
Patient: That sounds good. I'm really hoping this clears up soon.
Dr. Sophia Patel: I know it's uncomfortable, but it's important to address it early. I'll be in touch as soon as we have the results.
971:
Dr. James Anderson: You're 22 and experiencing chest pain? That's not something to mess around with, so let's dive right in. Can you tell me more about this chest pain?
Patient: It just started about an hour ago. I felt a sharp pain on the left side of my chest. But you know what? I remember this kind of thing happening once before a couple of months back with my nausea. It was very painful but after a day or so, everything went back to normal. So I figured it might be something similar this time.

Dr. James Anderson: I'm sorry to say, this doesn't exactly sound like that situation. But let's get you checked out properly, just to be sure. We do need tests and I think the best thing to do is start by looking at your chest.

Patient: Whatever you think is best, Doctor. I just hope it's not anything serious.

Dr. James Anderson: Well, let's hope for the best. Let's do some blood tests and get a chest X-ray just to be sure. We'll also conduct an EKG to make sure your heart is functioning well and we'll rule out anything that could be serious. Then we'll get your troponin levels to check for any heart damage. Trust me, we'll cover all our bases.

Patient: That all sounds good. What do I need to do?

Dr. James Anderson: You're just going to be here for a bit. We'll run the tests and I'll make sure you get the results as soon as possible. If this is still related to the pneumonia you were treated for you'll need a different set of antibiotics.
Patient: Yeah, when I last suffered with this, it was pneumonia. The doctors prescribed some medication then as well.
Dr. James Anderson: Okay, then I think those tests are even more important now. We need to be sure what were dealing with this time and if it's another case of pneumonia, I need to know why the antibiotics you were taking didn't work.
Patient: Yeah, I never really got over the whole thing so I've been feeling a bit off. I don't think it was properly treated either, there was just an awful lot going on back then but if it's another case of pneumonia then I'm certainly hoping I'll get something stronger this time round to fight it.

Dr. James Anderson: We'll see about that in a moment. Just wait here for me and we'll run those tests. As fast as we can, I promise you. Just try not to worry. We'll get this sorted.  If not you can book follow-up appointments later. The main priority is to figure out what's causing the chest pain but be assured if it was just due to pneumonia then it won't take long to diagnose and to get it treated. The EKG is just to be certain.
972:
Dr. Michael Davis: Alright, let's get this over with. You're here for a sore throat? Must be going around these days.

Patient: Yeah, it's been really sore for a few days now. It's making it hard to swallow and talk.

Dr. Michael Davis:  Any other symptoms? Fever, swollen glands, anything like that?

Patient: Actually, yeah, I've felt a bit feverish, and my glands are swollen on the side of my neck.

Dr. Michael Davis: Got it. We'll do a few tests, start with a throat culture to rule out some common infections. Youve got a history of some serious stuff in the past.

Patient: So I guess they've been pretty rough lately, you think this means I've got that mono thing going around again?

Dr. Michael Davis: Could be. We'll also do a complete blood count and a Monospot test to confirm. So have you got this diagnosis before? Must have been a pretty rough one.

Patient: Yeah, the first time was rough. Lots of fatigue, swollen lymph nodes, you know the deal. Second time was a little better.

Dr. Michael Davis: Alright, let's see what we're dealing with this time. Meanwhile we'll do the throat culture I mentioned. We'll use your blood to do a CBC and an antinuclear antibody test from your blood. Stay off your feet a bit and keep your fluids up. Avoid any physical activity or contact with others until we get results. I'll have the nurse bring you some water. Anything else bothering you? Chest pains, constipation again?

Patient: Not this time, thankfully. Just the sore throat and fever.

Dr. Michael Davis: Okay, hang in there the tests will give a clearer picture in a few days. It's something we can work with. I'll check back on you to help with the results.

Patient: Thanks, Doc. Appreciate it.

Dr. Michael Davis: Just do what I said.
973:
Dr. James Anderson: Alright, let's get started. What brings you in today?

Patient: Hey there! I've been having some issues with constipation. It's been going on for a few days now, and it's getting quite uncomfortable.

Dr. James Anderson: Let's figure this out then. How long have you been experiencing this?

Patient: Probably about a week. I've tried over-the-counter stuff, but it hasn't helped much.

Dr. James Anderson: Let's do some tests and go from there. We can start with a colonoscopy to get a better look at what's going on.

Patient: I understand, Doc. Let's do it.
974:
Dr. James Anderson: Hey there, what brings you in today?

 Patient: Hi Doc, I've been feeling a bit lightheaded lately. It comes and goes. I'm not sure. It's a bit odd really,

 Dr. James Anderson: Lightheadedness, huh? Alright, let's try to figure this out. When did it start?

 Patient: Just  the last couple of days. I was doing some college work.  I have a  few deadlines coming up. But it's fine, I can handle it.

 Dr. James Anderson: You might not have mentioned it before, but I see chemo on your medical records and I couldn't help but notice, you've been through some tough stuff already. How are you feeling now otherwise? Because I remember in some cases that people experience light headedness from side effects but its been a while since you last had chemo and most side effects would have cleared by now.

 Patient: Really, yes. It has been so long, I can barely remember it now!. I must admit, I'm feeling much better. The nausea is gone, and I've even gained some weight back, and the painkillers kept the pain at bay, so much so that there are days where I don't need them anymore.

 Dr. James Anderson: That's great to hear!! Do your teachers and classmates help you out a lot when doing big workloads? Because in my line of work we see people, with this condition experience lightheadedness when they over exert them selfs, have you over exerted yourself lately?

 Patient: Oh, I dunno. All I know is that I love to have a good laugh with everyone. I don't know about exerting myself too much. Well, actually, yes I do. I went out for a run this morning. But that's normal for me! It's my way of relieving stress.

 Dr. James Anderson: It's good to take walks and spend time with friends, but you might want to take it easy from time to time. There are ways to de-stress without overexerting yourself! Now I would probably want an EEG to run some tests. Would you be comfortable with that?

 Patient: I've never done them, but sure if it will help!

 Dr. James Anderson: Perfect. I'll order that for you, and hopefully, we'll get some answers. In the meantime, stay hydrated and take it easy. I know you're feeling good, but just be cautious, okay?

 Patient: Absolutely, Doc. Thanks for everything!

Dr. Ethan Wilson: Let's do a few tests to rule out any possible issues. We might need to do an electromyography to check your muscle function.
Patient: Sure, whatever you think is best. I just want to feel better.

Patient: Ok I will I am getting a little worried.

  Dr. Ethan Wilson: Let's take a look at some of your blood work. I want to rule out any underlying issues.

Dr. Hannah Johnson: Okay, let's do a few tests to get a better idea of what's going on. We'll start with an X-ray and an MRI.

Patient: No nothing else that i can think of at the moment.
Dr. Hannah Johnson: Ok that's fine. I'm going to run a few tests, i'll get our tech to run a stool test and a electrolyte test as well, they will take samples. How is everything else going with you today?

Patient: Yeah, it's been really bad. I've thrown up a few times. Nothing I've eaten stays down, and there's this constant feeling of nausea.

Dr. William Brown: How long has this been going on?

Dr. William Brown: Alright, I'm going to need to run some tests to figure out what's happening. We'll start with a CT scan and maybe an ultrasound. Also need to check your electrolyte levels, see if something like that is off. I'll send you over to radiology for the scan first.

Patient: Okay, sounds good. When can I get these done?

Dr. William Brown: They should be able to fit you in soon. After that, we'll adjust your treatment depending on what we find. You might need some tests repeated or more medications, but let's see what the scans show first. It's important you stay hydrated in the meantime.

Dr. James Anderson: So, what brings you in today?
Patient: Hey Doc, I've been dealing with this annoying fever. It's been hanging around for a bit now.
Dr. James Anderson: Ok, when did this fever start and what is the temperature?
Patient: I actually don't know the exact temperature, I just have a thermometer at home it's about 100 degrees. It's been on and off for a couple of days now.
Dr. James Anderson: Let's get it measured with our equipment here ok, and I will also suggest we get a urine test done, it will not take anytime, we can do it here in the ER.
Patient: Sure thing, doc. Not the first time I've dealt with a fever but this one's got me a bit worried.
Dr. James Anderson: That's one of the things I deal with here in my emergency department, do not worry we will figure out the problem. Why do you feel like it is worrying though?
Patient: I don't know, I have had a lot on my mental plate and i think it might be making it worse somehow.
Dr. James Anderson: A lot of our fevers are mental related, so I think you may be right about that, but let's see what the tests show before jumping to any conclusions. How has the rest of your health been?
Patient: I've been pretty out going for a man my age, no real issues that I could tell. A while ago I had a pretty bad issue, it was probably the worst time in my life.
Dr. James Anderson: Yes I remember.
Patient: I'm doing well now, back to my old self, but sometimes I get anxiety about it coming back.
Dr. James Anderson: It is normal to have anxiety about it coming back, however you can't let it affect your life, let's deal with the present. So do you have any other symptoms besides the fever?
Patient: Not really, just feeling a bit under the weather. Nothing too serious.
Dr. James Anderson: Ok, that's good. I think we just need to rule out a few things first and foremost let's address this fever.  I think the most likely thing is that the fever is in response to something you ate.
Patient: Yeah, maybe. I've been eating out a lot lately.

Patient: Nope, just felt nauseous a couple of times.

Patient: Yeah, doc, it started a few hours ago. I've been feeling a bit lightheaded too.
Dr. Michael Davis: Any history of this happening before?
Patient: Not really, except maybe a few times when I pushed myself too hard at the gym. But this feels different.
Dr. Michael Davis: Let's check your oxygen levels. Take this pulse oximeter and place it on your fingertip. Anything else been bothering you?
Patient: Not really, but I've had some issues in the past. A couple of years back, I had some really bad nausea and vomiting. Turned out to be a tumor, but I'm in remission now.

Patient: Oh yeah, I had a fever a while back that turned into proteinuria. Had to take some diuretics for that, still on 'em.
Dr. Michael Davis: Got it. Let's get you some air and see if this settles down. You have a history of a tumor, so we need to keep an eye on that. Have you been taking your medications regularly?
Patient: Like clockwork, doc. I know how important it is.
Dr. Michael Davis: Good. Let's run a few more tests just to be safe. In the meantime, try to relax and take deep breaths.

Dr. Michael Davis: We'll figure this out. Just hang in there.

Dr. Abigail Thompson: I understand that's frightening. Do you have any history of heart issues or high blood pressure?
Patient: Well, a bit of high blood pressure, but not too high.
Dr. Abigail Thompson:  Your previous visits have shown various issues, but nothing too concerning. Alright, let's get you in for some tests to figure out what's going on. We might run an MRI and an EEG to get a better look at things. Can you remember anything that happened just before you fainted?
Patient: Not really. It was sudden.
Dr. Abigail Thompson: Okay, it does seem a bit unpredictable. We'll do our best to get to the bottom of this. Just be straightforward with me—any other symptoms at all lately?
Patient: No, not really. I've had some bouts of nausea last year, and it wasn't fun, but that's pretty much it. I don't have any family history of medical issues.
Dr. Abigail Thompson: So a bit of nausea, and now this. We'll rule out anything serious, don't worry. Let's start with those tests. By the way, anything that makes you feel better, just let me or any of the nurses know and we'll get it sorted.

Patient: Yes, it's been bothering me for about a week now. I haven't been able to sleep well because of it.

Dr. William Brown: I see. Let's start with the basics. Have you had a fever?

Patient: No, my temperature has been normal. I've been checking it regularly.

Dr. William Brown: Good. And what about shortness of breath or wheezing?

Dr. William Brown: Given the information I have and ruling out other things, we're still left with what we started with.

Patient: But it doesn't feel like a normal cough. I have this nagging feeling that something else might be wrong.

Dr. William Brown: Well, you're here now and given you aren't getting better you need to have a course of antibiotics.

Dr. William Brown: Listen it is worth noting that you're not the first person to have a cough like this. This cough is something that you've been dealing with. Keep an eye on it, and if it worsens or you develop new symptoms, come back in.

987:

We can treat it with aspirin while we wait for the results. Lets get the tests done in the meantime and if it gets better then we may not need further investigation.

Patient: No, just the pain. I've not had any fever, nausea, or vomiting.
